posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked: The fill, color, and capsule all look perfectly fine. The neck label is the correct neck label. The main label falling off isn’t remotely surprising. Verset labels are apparently attached to the bottle with a single molecule of the same cheap glue George Costanza got for his wedding invitation envelopes. Every single one I’ve ever had in my cellar has come loose, it’s a miracle the neck label stayed on. Drink and enjoy.

posts.name: Mike Leigh
posts.cooked: Keep us posted as to how it is. Hopefully it’s just as magical as advertised.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked: I hope it drinks as well as the last couple I had. When it’s on, one of the greatest bottles of Syrah I’ve ever had. I don’t know how a wine evokes a bonfire for me, but the 99 verset does.

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked: I would have taken that calculated risk. My prediction is that it’s superb. Enjoy!

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
Take the foil off and check the cork for branding.

The neck tag isn’t straight, so it may have been glued back on.

FWIW, labels tend to fall off due to dry conditions.

posts.name: N@th@n V@nd€rgrift
posts.cooked:
Keith_Levenberg:

The fill, color, and capsule all look perfectly fine. The neck label is the correct neck label. The main label falling off isn’t remotely surprising. Verset labels are apparently attached to the bottle with a single molecule of the same cheap glue George Costanza got for his wedding invitation envelopes. Every single one I’ve ever had in my cellar has come loose, it’s a miracle the neck label stayed on. Drink and enjoy.

Agree with Keith, especially about the glue. It looks like my 1999s, where one has a protruding cork. The wine was purchased by me, on release, was imported by Kermit so it’s probably about as good as provenance gets.

That being said, it’s hard to trust anything these days when it hasn’t been in my custody.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Agree. A good bottle is astounding. @Jay_Miller may have opened the absolute best one I’ve had—at a dinner in late 2016. Amazing bottle also from @John_Morris not too long after that that took a good hour in the decanter to get going.

But there’s been some variability in other bottles, to be expected.

posts.name: Zachary Ross
posts.cooked: 1999 Verset is amazing. Great buy!

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Jayson_Cohen:

Agree. A good bottle is astounding. @Jay_Miller may have opened the absolute best one I’ve had—at a dinner in late 2016. Amazing bottle also from @John_Morris not too long after that that took a good hour in the decanter to get going.

Yes, I decanted that bottle (also at Jay’s) an hour or two ahead, and it needed all of that – and some time in the glass. But it was superb when it opened up.

As Keith said, no need to worry about the label. Lots of wines with hand-applied labels with simple glue come off, particularly if they were stored in high humidity cellars.

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked:
Appreciate the positivity. I did see that there is clear bottle variation on tasting notes where some bottles are nearly life changing and some merely okay. So that was a part of the calculated risk. The other risk obviously being whether this is in fact a Noel Verset 1999 Cornas.

Funny enough this was from a charity auction with no tax or buyer’s premium and the lot title and description was Alain Verset 1999 Cornas, but after some preliminary research I eliminated the possibility that it could be Alain. I don’t think there were any Alain Verset Cornas in 1999 at all in fact. Some research showed another Verset producer existing in 1999 (Louis), but wa never exported and I can’t find pictures to compare neck label and capsule to eliminate that possibility. As mentioned the neck label and capsule quite clearly match Noel Verset. I will remove the capsule on receiving the bottle and check the cork.

Opening bid was $1500 CAD, but in the last 24 hours without an opener they dropped the open to $750 CAD. I hopped in a won on the second bid at $850 CAD ($620 USD). A total bargain compared to market price if it is in fact authentic (and especially if the bottle is “on”). Though I am cognizant many of you on here have bought this for 1/10 of the price ~15 years ago. In my mind the price is worth it to have the reference point stuff. Half way between what I paid for the 1991 and 1983 Chave recently and I’m hoping for an experience somewhere in that ballpark in terms of quality, but buying at around 1/2 of market rice eases the pain of it not quite being right.
posts.display_username: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked:
Basically, yes it is. W-S Low at $1395 USD.

$1500 CAD open with no tax or premium, then a hammer at $850 CAD is a relative bargain, but still way more than these used to go for. Maybe there are more cost effective ways to have a beautiful, mature, traditional Northern Rhone experience than the $850 CAD this hammered but they’re seemingly more and more rare.

Mature Jamet now lists in a similar price range to this hammer. I had a source for mature Jasmin that was a little cheaper but that has dried up and W-S listings are also now in this same range. I don’t see a single W-S listing for mature Levet. Allemand also in this range. Any other ideas for someone not terribly deep in N Rhone?
